title: Advanced Metaprogramming & Hooks
subtitle: Bend the interpreter at runtime — descriptors, decorators, metaclasses, and context managers that build and police your types for you.
goal: Reach under attribute access and class creation: write data descriptors that validate, class decorators and metaclasses that synthesize and register types, and context managers that guarantee cleanup and rollback — the machinery behind ORMs, dataclasses, and trading-system guard rails.

blocks.heading: Attribute access is a protocol you can intercept
blocks.html:
obj.x is not a dictionary lookup — it is a protocol. When the class defines x as a descriptor (an object implementing __get__/__set__/__delete__), every read and write of obj.x is routed through that descriptor. This is the mechanism behind property, classmethod, staticmethod, ORM columns, and typed fields.

A data descriptor (defines __set__ or __delete__) takes precedence over the instance __dict__; a non-data descriptor (only __get__) is shadowed by an instance attribute of the same name. That precedence rule is the single most important thing to know here.

blocks.caption: A validating data descriptor with __set_name__
blocks.explain: __set_name__ fires once at class-creation time and tells the descriptor the attribute name it was bound to, so it can pick a private slot. Because __set__ is defined, this is a data descriptor: it intercepts EVERY write to a.balance, even though balance lives on the class, not the instance.

blocks.html: Data vs non-data precedence. If Positive defined only __get__, then a.balance = 5 would write straight into a.__dict__ and shadow the descriptor forever. Defining __set__ (even one that just stores) is what makes validation un-bypassable.

blocks.heading: property is just a descriptor — and so is every method
blocks.html: property is a built-in data descriptor whose __get__ calls your getter. Functions are non-data descriptors: obj.method triggers the function's __get__, which returns a bound method with self pre-filled. Understanding this collapses a dozen 'magic' behaviors into one rule.

blocks.caption: A class decorator that synthesizes __init__ and __repr__
blocks.explain: A class decorator receives the fully-built class and returns a (usually mutated) class. Reading __annotations__ lets you generate boilerplate from a declarative spec — this is a miniature version of how dataclasses works.

blocks.heading: Metaclasses: the class of a class
blocks.html: If a class is an object, what is its type? By default, type. A metaclass subclasses type and customizes class creation (__new__/__init__) and class calls (__call__, which runs when you instantiate). Use them when you must affect every subclass uniformly — registration, validation of method signatures, automatic IDs. For most jobs a class decorator or __init_subclass__ is lighter; reach for a metaclass when you need to intercept instantiation itself.

blocks.caption: A metaclass that stamps every instance with an auto ID
blocks.explain: metaclass __call__ wraps instantiation: it runs type.__call__ (which calls __new__ then __init__) and then post-processes the object. Each class gets its own _next because __init__ runs once per class definition.

blocks.question: A class defines `x = D()` where D implements only __get__ (a non-data descriptor). You then run `obj.x = 5`. What does `obj.x` return afterward?
options: 5 — the instance attribute shadows the non-data descriptor
blocks.explain: Non-data descriptors (only __get__) are overridden by instance __dict__. Writing obj.x = 5 puts 5 in the instance dict, and subsequent reads find it before consulting the class descriptor. Only DATA descriptors (with __set__/__delete__) take precedence over the instance.

blocks.heading: Context managers guarantee the exit runs
blocks.html:
A context manager pairs setup with teardown that is guaranteed to run — on normal exit and on exception. The protocol is two dunders: __enter__ (returns the value bound by as) and __exit__(exc_type, exc, tb). If __exit__ returns a truthy value, the exception is suppressed. That single return value is how you implement rollback, retries, and 'swallow and log'.

contextlib.contextmanager lets you write the same thing as a generator: everything before yield is enter, everything after (in a finally) is exit.

blocks.caption: A transactional context manager with rollback
blocks.code:
class Transaction:
    def __init__(self, target):
        self.target = target
    def __enter__(self):
        self._snap = list(self.target)   # snapshot
        return self.target
    def __exit__(self, et, e, tb):
        if et is not None:
            self.target[:] = self._snap  # rollback
            return True                  # suppress the error
        return False                     # commit

log = []
with Transaction(log) as L:
    L.append(1); L.append(2)
with Transaction(log) as L:
    L.append(99)
    raise ValueError('boom')             # rolled back + suppressed
print(log)                               # [1, 2]
blocks.explain: The second block appends 99 then raises; __exit__ sees the exception, restores the snapshot (dropping 99), and returns True so the ValueError never escapes. This is the exact shape of a DB transaction or an order-batch that must be all-or-nothing.

blocks.html: __getattr__ vs __getattribute__. __getattr__ is a fallback called only when normal lookup fails — cheap and safe. __getattribute__ intercepts every access and is a loaded footgun: touch self.anything inside it without delegating to super().__getattribute__ and you get infinite recursion.

blocks.title: Write a timer context manager
blocks.instructions: Make a class-based context manager `Tag` whose __enter__ prints `start` and __exit__ prints `end`. Use it in a `with` block.
blocks.passMsg: That's the protocol — enter sets up, exit always runs.

problems.title: Un-bypassable validated field (data descriptor)
problems.instructions:
Implement a data descriptor Positive used as Account.balance. It must reject any value ≤ 0.

Input: first line is an integer N; the next N lines are commands. SET x attempts to set the balance to integer x; GET queries it.

Output: for each GET print the current balance, or UNSET if it was never successfully set. For each SET with a value ≤ 0 print REJECT (a successful SET prints nothing).
problems.starter:
import sys

class Positive:
    """TODO: make this a DATA descriptor (define __get__ AND __set__).
    Enforce value > 0; store the value per-instance (e.g. in "_" + name)."""
    def __set_name__(self, owner, name):
        self.slot = "_" + name
    # TODO: __get__(self, obj, owner=None) -> return stored value or None
    # TODO: __set__(self, obj, value) -> raise ValueError(value) if value <= 0

class Account:
    balance = Positive()

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split("\n")
    n = int(data[0])
    acc = Account()
    out = []
    for i in range(1, n + 1):
        parts = data[i].split()
        if not parts:
            continue
        if parts[0] == "GET":
            # TODO: print "UNSET" if never set, else the value
            pass
        elif parts[0] == "SET":
            # TODO: try to set acc.balance = int(parts[1]); on ValueError append "REJECT"
            pass
    sys.stdout.write("\n".join(out) + "\n")

main()

tests.name: zero + multi-reject
tests.stdin:
6
SET 0
GET
SET 42
SET -1
GET
GET

problems.hint: Define __set__ so the descriptor is a DATA descriptor; raise ValueError for value <= 0 and store into '_' + name.

problems.title: Declarative model via class decorator
problems.instructions:
Write a class decorator @model that reads cls.__annotations__ and synthesizes an __init__(self, *args) (assigning positionally in annotation order) and a __repr__ of the form Point(x=1, y=2) (using repr() of each value).

Input: first line N; next N lines each contain two integers x y.

Output: for each line, print repr(Point(x, y)).
problems.starter:
import sys

def model(cls):
    """TODO: read cls.__annotations__ (field order) and synthesize:
      - __init__(self, *args) assigning args to the annotated fields in order
      - __repr__ returning 'ClassName(x=<x>, y=<y>)' using repr() of each value
    Return cls."""
    fields = list(cls.__annotations__)
    # TODO: define and attach cls.__init__ and cls.__repr__
    return cls

@model
class Point:
    x: int
    y: int

def main():
    lines = sys.stdin.read().split("\n")
    n = int(lines[0])
    out = []
    for i in range(1, n + 1):
        a, b = lines[i].split()
        out.append(repr(Point(int(a), int(b))))
    sys.stdout.write("\n".join(out) + "\n")

main()

tests.name: basic
tests.expectStdout:
Point(x=1, y=2)
Point(x=-4, y=7)
Point(x=0, y=0)

problems.hint: fields = list(cls.__annotations__); attach generated __init__ and __repr__ as cls attributes, then return cls.

problems.title: Per-class auto IDs via metaclass
problems.instructions:
Write a metaclass AutoID that gives every instance an auto-incrementing id attribute, counted independently per class, starting at 0. Two classes Order and Trade use it.

Input: first line N; next N lines each name a class to instantiate (Order or Trade).

Output: for each, print Name#id (e.g. Order#0).
problems.hint: Initialize cls._next = 0 in the metaclass __init__ (runs once per class), and override __call__ to stamp obj.id then increment cls._next.

problems.title: All-or-nothing batches (context manager rollback)
problems.instructions:
Write a class-based context manager Transaction(target_list). On __enter__ snapshot the list; on __exit__, if an exception occurred inside the block, restore the snapshot (rollback) and suppress the exception; otherwise commit.

Each input batch is appended to a shared list inside a with Transaction(state) block. A 0 in a batch raises mid-append, so that whole batch must roll back.

Input: first line N; next N lines are space-separated integers (one batch per line).

Output: the final list contents, space-separated on one line.
problems.starter:
import sys

class Transaction:
    """TODO: a class-based context manager wrapping a list `target`.
    __enter__: snapshot a copy of target, return target.
    __exit__: if an exception occurred inside the block, restore the
    snapshot (rollback) and return True to SUPPRESS it; otherwise commit."""
    def __init__(self, target):
        self.target = target
    # TODO: __enter__(self)
    # TODO: __exit__(self, exc_type, exc, tb)

def main():
    lines = sys.stdin.read().split("\n")
    n = int(lines[0])
    state = []
    for i in range(1, n + 1):
        nums = [int(x) for x in lines[i].split()]
        with Transaction(state) as s:
            for v in nums:
                if v == 0:
                    raise ValueError("poison")
                s.append(v)
    sys.stdout.write(" ".join(map(str, state)) + "\n")

main()
problems.hint: __exit__ must return True when exc_type is not None (after restoring the snapshot) to swallow the error; return False on clean exit to commit.
